sections.heading: BARMM is low on every antenatal care measure
sections.body: BARMM is the clearest regional outlier in 2025, not just on one indicator but across the whole antenatal care sequence. Skilled antenatal care was 57.2 percent, 4+ visits were 43.3 percent, 8+ visits were 14.0 percent, iron supplementation was 62.6 percent, and neonatal tetanus protection was 54.8 percent. Other regions may be low on one part of care, but BARMM sits at the bottom of all five measures. That makes the regional gap a broad service-contact and continuity problem rather than a single-indicator weakness.

sections.heading: More contact does not always mean enough visits
sections.body: Several regions have high skilled antenatal care but much lower completion of 8+ visits. Caraga had 87.9 percent skilled antenatal care and 94.3 percent with 4+ visits, yet only 29.3 percent reached 8+ visits. Davao Region had 90.9 percent skilled antenatal care and 91.1 percent with 4+ visits, but 8+ visits were 27.3 percent. The regional pattern suggests that early or basic contact is much more common than the fuller visit schedule.

sections.heading: The strongest gains are not in the same indicator
sections.body: The largest regional gains from 2022 to 2025 appear in different parts of the antenatal care pathway. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ had the biggest increase in skilled antenatal care, rising 16.4 points from 70.7 to 87.1 percent. BARMM had the biggest gain in 4+ visits, up 15.4 points from 27.9 to 43.3 percent, while Central Visayas had the biggest gain in 8+ visits, up 29.2 points from 35.1 to 64.3 percent. Progress was therefore spread across different regions depending on which part of care is measured.

sections.heading: Neonatal tetanus protection weakened in several high-contact regions
sections.body: Some regions with strong antenatal contact did not show the same strength in neonatal tetanus protection. Eastern Visayas still had 93.0 percent skilled antenatal care in 2025, but neonatal tetanus protection fell from 94.9 percent in 2022 to 77.2 percent in 2025. Cagayan Valley also had 88.4 percent skilled antenatal care, while neonatal tetanus protection declined from 94.4 to 81.2 percent. This divergence matters because tetanus protection is not simply tracking the overall level of antenatal contact.

sections.heading: The national shift is mainly in 8+ visits
sections.body: At the national level, antenatal care changed most clearly in the intensity of visits. For live births and stillbirths, skilled antenatal care was 87.3 percent in 2025, 4+ visits were 84.8 percent, and iron supplementation was 87.7 percent, all only modestly higher than in 2022. The 8+ visit measure rose much more, from 29.1 to 39.9 percent. The main national change is therefore not whether women had any skilled contact, but whether more pregnancies reached the higher visit threshold.

sections.heading: Teen mothers remain lower on the later parts of care
sections.body: Mothers younger than 20 had lower antenatal care results than older mothers across the 2025 measures. Skilled antenatal care was 84.6 percent for mothers under 20, compared with 87.8 percent for ages 20 to 34 and 86.8 percent for ages 35 to 49. The gap is sharper for 8+ visits, where mothers under 20 were at 25.8 percent compared with 42.8 percent among ages 20 to 34. Neonatal tetanus protection was also lower for mothers under 20 at 69.4 percent.

sections.heading: Urban advantage is concentrated in visit intensity
sections.body: Urban-rural differences are present in 2025, but they are not equally large across all antenatal care measures. Skilled antenatal care differed by 2.6 points, with urban areas at 88.6 percent and rural areas at 86.0 percent. The gap was much larger for 8+ visits, where urban areas reached 47.3 percent compared with 32.6 percent in rural areas. Residence therefore matters most for the higher visit threshold, not simply for whether a skilled provider was reached.

sections.heading: Education separates basic contact from sustained care
sections.body: Educational differences are wide in 2025, especially when looking beyond initial antenatal contact. Skilled antenatal care was 95.0 percent among mothers with college education, compared with 49.8 percent among mothers with no education and 76.1 percent among those with Grades 1-6. For 8+ visits, the contrast was also large: 53.2 percent for college and 54.2 percent for post-secondary, compared with 24.1 percent for no education and 14.7 percent for Grades 1-6. The education pattern shows that the biggest divide is not only contact with care, but also the ability to continue through more visits.

sections.heading: Wealth gaps widen at the 8+ visit threshold
sections.body: The wealth gradient is visible across antenatal care, but it becomes much steeper for 8+ visits. Skilled antenatal care was 95.6 percent in the highest wealth quintile and 80.0 percent in the lowest. For 8+ visits, the highest quintile reached 67.5 percent, while the lowest was only 20.7 percent. The richest-lowest gap is therefore 15.6 points for skilled care but 46.8 points for 8+ visits, showing that sustained care is where wealth differences become most pronounced.

takeaways: National antenatal care levels are high for skilled contact and 4+ visits, but 8+ visits remain much lower.
Regional variation is sharpest where contact needs to continue, with BARMM lowest across all five antenatal care measures.
The largest 2022-to-2025 gains are split across different regions and indicators rather than concentrated in one regional story.
Education, wealth, and residence gaps are most pronounced for 8+ visits, showing that sustained care is less evenly distributed than basic contact.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
